summary: Drawing on Edwin Black's 'IBM and the Holocaust,' Cantrill argues that the real risk of AI is not autonomous destruction of humanity but human abuse of technology for profit and power. He traces how IBM's punch card technology served as a force-multiplier for Nazi genocide, driven by CEO Thomas Watson's singular devotion to profit. Cantrill rejects both AI doomers who want to prohibit development and 'effective accelerationists' who refuse to consider any negative consequences, arguing instead that we should enforce existing laws against companies that abuse technology rather than trying to regulate the technology itself.
key_points: IBM's punch card technology was essential to Nazi information management, enabling organization of the Holocaust
Thomas Watson's sole motivation was profit — he dealt with Nazis as business partners and only distanced himself when it became commercially necessary
Technology is a force-multiplier on humanity for both good and ill, from punch cards to radio to social media
AI doomerism distracts from real risks of human abuse of technology
Both prohibition and uncritical acceleration are wrong approaches to AI risk
Enforcing existing laws against corporate abuse is the pragmatic middle path
money_quotes: Technology serves as a force-multiplier on humanity, for both better and ill.
The fears of AI autonomously destroying humanity are worse than nonsense, because they distract us from the very real possibilities of how AI may be abused.

To allow ourselves to even contemplate a prohibition of the development of certain kinds of computer programs is to delude ourselves into thinking that the problem is a technical problem rather than a human one.

IBM knew which machines were where (and knew, for example, that concentration camps all had ample sorters and tabulators), to the point that the company was able to retrieve them all after the war – along with the profits that the machines had earned.

Even though the specifics of the technologies we work on may be without precedent, the humanity they serve to advance endures across generations; those who fret about the future would be well advised to learn from the past!
key_insight: The true danger of AI is not the technology becoming autonomous but humans wielding it without accountability — and history, from IBM's Holocaust complicity to Facebook's role in the Rohingya genocide, shows that enforcing laws against corporate abuse matters far more than trying to regulate the technology itself.
